detailedExemplarData.title: Finding the Optimal Shape for a 1.5 Litre Plastic Bottle

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear step-by-step logical progression with appropriate headings and sub-headings
Comprehensive table of contents and numbered figures aiding navigation
Concise focus on minimising surface area with only occasional minor digressions
weaknesses: Figure captions out of sequence and duplicated in places, disrupting visual flow
Minor awkward line breaks and cut-off sentences at page transitions
Inconsistent citation style in the references list
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and well organized with a clear introduction, body and conclusion, and the majority of content directly supports the aim (band 3). Minor issues (repeated remarks on practicality, caption misplacements and citation inconsistencies) prevent it reaching the top band of exceptional seamlessness, justifying a score of 3 out of 4.

strengths: Consistent and mostly accurate use of mathematical language and notation throughout
Effective variety of representations (algebraic derivations, Desmos graphs, bar chart) to illustrate key results
Logical structure of explanations and clear linkage between steps and conclusions
weaknesses: Graph axes and bar-chart axes lack labels and scales, limiting standalone interpretability
Occasional undefined variables (e.g., reused âhâ without redefinition) and missing units in numerical results
Intermediate steps in some quotientârule applications are omitted, reducing transparency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite a few lapses in labels and stepwise detail, the student employs a diverse set of mathematical representations effectively and maintains clear logical communication (band 4). The minor notation and labelling weaknesses are not sufficient to demote it to band 3, supporting a score of 4 out of 4.

strengths: Clear personal motivation (reducing plastic waste) articulated and reinforced by imagery
Evidence of independent thinking in combining cylinder and cone models to optimise packaging
Good creativity in extending the investigation to a composite bottle rather than singleâshape analysis
weaknesses: Methods remain standard SL optimisation techniques lacking the breakthrough innovation required for band 3
Personal approach, while genuine, does not introduce sufficiently novel lines of inquiry
Creativity and initiative are solid but not sustained at an exceptional, standout level
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows clear personal engagement with significant evidence of independent thinking and creativity (band 2). However, it stops short of outstanding originality or highly innovative approaches, meriting 2 out of 3.

strengths: Substantial critical reflection on practical and manufacturing constraints (wall thickness, usability)
Quantitative evaluation of outcomes (22.155 % plastic reduction) linking results back to the aim
Suggestions for future extensions (e.g., stress tests under real-world conditions) demonstrate forward-looking critique
weaknesses: Rounding impacts on volume calculations are not fully explored
Some reflective comments are spread thinly rather than consolidated in a deeper discussion
Evaluation of limitations lacks a small illustrative diagram to justify certain assumptions (e.g., cap volume)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides substantial critical reflection throughout, with thoughtful analysis of results, clear evaluation of implications and credible suggestions for future work, fitting band 3. While a few details could be deepened, the level of insight and critique justifies 3 out of 3.

strengths: Relevant mathematics is used comprehensively across a wide range of shapes (cone, cylinder, sphere, hemisphere, composite)
Mathematical techniques (derivatives, quotient rule, power rule) are applied skillfully and correctly in context
Derivation of composite relationships and efficient selection of methods (e.g., skip differentiation for sphere) demonstrate judicious command of calculus
weaknesses: Minor algebraic inconsistency in the slant-height formula and occasional typographical errors
Units formatting is at times inconsistent, and parentheses are missing in complex formulae
No application of higher-level techniques (e.g., Lagrange multipliers) that could further enrich the investigation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration employs relevant SL-level mathematics effectively and largely without error, demonstrating thorough understanding and mostly correct work. The breadth and depth of application go beyond basic course expectations but stop short of expert flawlesslyâcorrect performance, supporting a score of 5 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations accompany each calculation, and conclusions follow logically from results.  While sentence structure is occasionally informal, the reasoning is clear and mathematically sound, corresponding to good logical structure without the polished precision of band 4.

Derivatives, algebraic manipulation and numerical substitutions are largely correct, demonstrating good understanding.  A few minor inaccuracies (e.g., mis-typed formula for slant height) are present but do not materially affect conclusions, indicating âgood understanding and mostly correctâ accuracy rather than the flawless work required for bands 5â6.

The student employs algebraic formulae, derivative working, Desmos graphs and a comparative bar chart.  These multiple forms are clear and generally accurate, though the graph axes lack labels and scales, preventing the work from reaching the âhighly effectiveâ description.

The exploration moves step-by-step from a motivating context, through individual shape analyses, to an optimisation of a composite bottle.  Headings, sub-headings and transitions make the progression easy to follow and no major logical gaps are present.  Minor digressions (e.g. repeated comments about practicality) occasionally interrupt the flow, preventing the work from reaching the exceptionally seamless quality of the top band.

Creating and comparing several models, then formulating an optimised composite shape, shows creativity and initiative beyond a single-shape study.  The modelling remains relatively straightforward, so the level of creativity is good but not exceptional.

Results are analysed (e.g., percentage plastic saved, comparison chart) and linked back to the aim.  The critique remains at a thoughtful rather than âdeep and insightfulâ level; manufacturing constraints are mentioned but not rigorously explored.

The student evaluates the optimised model against a standard bottle and quantifies savings, acknowledging limitations and suggesting extensions.  While comprehensive future implications are not fully developed, the evaluation is clear and relevant, meriting band 2.

The student reflects on practicality, environmental impact and the trade-off between efficiency and usability at several points, offering meaningful but not deeply critical reflection.  Discussion is present throughout but lacks the depth and probing questions characteristic of the top band.

Mathematical symbols (Ï, r, h, l) and calculus notation are mostly correct and used consistently.  Minor slips such as âS. Aâ without definition and the casual use of commas instead of multiplication signs appear but do not impede understanding, aligning with âmostly consistentâ usage.

The student articulates a clear personal motivation (reducing plastic waste) and makes design decisions (e.g., excluding the cap volume) that demonstrate a personal approach.  Nevertheless, these choices are expected within the task context and not âhighly innovative,â placing the work solidly in band 2.

Most content directly supports the aim of minimising surface area for a fixed volume.  Occasional repetition of general environmental commentary and some over-long algebraic working add unnecessary length, but the majority of the 21 pages remain relevant, giving a concise overall impression consistent with band 3.

All mathematics used (surface-area formulae, volume relationships, differentiation, graphical verification) is directly relevant to the optimisation problem, and each method is applied effectively to progress the investigation.  Coverage is substantial but not truly comprehensive across advanced topics, fitting band 4.

The choice of topic is personal and the decision to combine cylinder and cone shows the studentâs own line of inquiry, evidencing significant independent thinking.  However, methods remain standard SL optimisation techniques, falling short of the outstanding originality required for band 3.

A clear introduction, body (shapes analysed one after another) and conclusion are provided, supported by a table of contents and numbered figures.  Pages are clearly laid out and formulae/diagrams appear close to the text that references them.  A few pages have awkward line breaks and duplicated captions, so the structure is very good rather than exemplary.

Differential calculus and optimisation are fully commensurate with SL AA level; the student applies these correctly to a variety of shapes.  No mathematics below the course level dominates, yet the work does not extend to higher-level techniques (e.g., Lagrange multipliers), so band 4 is most appropriate.

annotations.comment: Reported values (e.g., â789 cm^2â and â7.97 cmâ) demonstrate clear numerical interpretation; ensure consistent unit formatting throughout.
Applying the quotient rule and power rule to derive $$f'(r)$$ shows strong command of calculus techniques.
The variable âhâ is reused from the cone section without redefinition; clarify variable definitions when switching contexts to avoid confusion.
The sequence of derivative tests (first then second) is logically structured, facilitating clear identification of minima.
Intermediate steps in the quotient rule application are omitted; include brief annotations on each termâs differentiation to improve transparency.
Presenting the surface areas of cone and cylinder side-by-side gives strong comparative insight and reinforces the investigationâs logical clarity.
When concluding that the cylinder is more efficient, reference the percentage difference calculated later to strengthen the comparative argument.
Efficient use of sphere formulas without differentiation is appropriate for a single-variable context, showing judicious method selection.
Figure 5âs caption is out of sequence; renumber figures consecutively to maintain consistency and avoid reader confusion.
Analyzing the hemisphere as a half-sphere adds depth and broadens the investigationâs scope beyond standard shapes.
The practical reflection on hemisphere limitations is insightful; expand this into a brief discussion of trade-offs at this stage.
Connecting the cubeâs storage advantages to its high surface area shows strong personal engagement with real-world design constraints.
The clear hierarchy of headings (Mathematics AA SL; Internal Assessment; Investigation title) establishes context effectively and supports reader orientation.
Consider adding a brief subtitle or summary sentence below the main title to preview the investigationâs aim and improve initial coherence.
The table of contents is comprehensive and correctly maps section titles to page numbers, aiding navigation.
Some entries appear stacked closely; adding spacing or separators between items would improve visual clarity and prevent page-number misreads.
The studentâs personal motivation for reducing plastic waste is articulated clearly, demonstrating genuine engagement.
The aim statement is cut off mid-sentence at the page break; ensure complete sentences are maintained between pages to preserve logical flow.
Embedding the plastic pollution image grounds the problem in a real-world context, reinforcing the investigationâs relevance.
The caption for Figure 1 interrupts the narrative; consider repositioning or formatting captions consistently to maintain text flow.
The derivation of $$h = \frac{4500}{\pi r^2}$$ from the volume constraint is correctly executed and clearly presented.
In the slant-height formula, the denominator inside the square root should be $(\pi r^2)^2$ rather than $\pi^2$, leading to an algebraic inconsistency.
The plan to graph the surface area function on Desmos bridges analytical and technological methods, enriching the investigation.
Axes in the Desmos screenshot (Figure 3) lack labels and units, making it difficult to interpret the plotted function values.
The cubeâs surface area result omits units in â786.2â; include âcmÂ²â consistently to avoid ambiguity.
The procedure for determining the cylinder-to-cone transition point lacks a visual aid; include a Desmos screenshot or diagram to illustrate how 21.2 cm was chosen.
Clarify the rationale for subtracting 4 cmÂ³ for the cap volume; a brief diagram or note would justify this assumption and improve transparency.
Deriving the composite relationships (e.g., $h=2r$, $h=2.84r$) synthesizes earlier results effectively and supports the new modelâs construction.
When solving for $r=3.67$ and $h=10.423$, discuss the impact of rounding on the total volume to demonstrate critical evaluation of accuracy.
Total surface-area calculation for the composite bottle is thorough and applies multiple formulae accurately.
The formula for the standard bottleâs surface area is densely written and missing parentheses; rewrite it in stepwise form to enhance readability.
Calculating a 22.155 % plastic reduction connects mathematical results to tangible environmental impact.
The bar chart comparing all shapes visualizes outcomes effectively, but it lacks axis labels and a legend for standalone interpretation.
The conclusion synthesizes mathematical findings with practical constraints, demonstrating strong reflection on real-world design trade-offs.
The final sentence on page 18 is truncated (âperfectâ¦â); ensure all reflections are complete to avoid losing critical insights.
Reflection on manufacturing constraints and wall thickness deepens critical analysis and connects mathematics to practical implications.
Consider evaluating limitations of the mathematical model under real-world stresses (e.g., pressure, drops) to deepen critical reflection.
References list includes varied sources but citation style is inconsistent; apply a single format (e.g., APA) for uniformity.
Verify all web links are active and include access dates consistently to strengthen academic rigor.
